name: Hindley-Milner Inference
summary: Static typing with parametric polymorphism and automatic type inference for a rank-1 polymorphic functional core. Algorithm W threads substitutions while recursively inferring expressions and unifying constraints; gives let-polymorphism, principal types, and predictable inference. The ML family popularized it; modern checkers extend it bidirectionally or constraint-based when subtyping/effects/GADTs join in.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Hindley-Milner inference provides let-polymorphism, principal types, and predictable inference for a rank-1 polymorphic functional core. Algorithm W (Damas-Milner, 1982) threads substitutions while recursively inferring expressions and unifying constraints.

The ML family popularized static typing with parametric polymorphism and automatic type inference, now commonly called Hindley-Milner. The rank-1 fragment — type abstractions only at the outermost level of let-bindings — is the largest fragment of System F that admits decidable inference without programmer-written type arguments.

sources.label: Damas and Milner — Principal type-schemes for functional programs

sections.title: Historical context — from ALGOL/Simula to ML
sections.content:
Hindley-Milner sits at the end of a 1960s–1970s lineage. ALGOL-era languages used types primarily as a storage and operation discipline; Simula made class-like abstractions part of the type story; the typed lambda calculus brought formal vocabulary into language implementation. ML combined static typing, parametric polymorphism, and type inference in a practical language used as the metalanguage of LCF, with System F as the underlying account of explicit type abstraction and Hindley's and Milner's independent inference results making a useful rank-1 fragment practical without explicit type arguments.

A broader survey of the type-theory-and-programming-languages lineage notes how ALGOL, Simula, typed lambda calculi, polymorphism, and Curry-Howard connections shaped the modern concept of type systems. Cardelli's lecture notes are the canonical compact introduction to the type-systems vocabulary that HM presupposes.

sources.label: A historical survey of type theory and programming languages (arXiv 1510.03726)

sections.title: Strengths
Costs and when HM stops being enough
